---
title: "4. The Two-Stage TMLE method"
date-modified: last-modified
---

::: {.callout-note .draft}
**Draft scaffold.** Need: full derivation following §9.3 of the strategy
doc + Box 1 / Box 2; redraw Figure 5 inline; add a short video that
walks through Stage 1 then Stage 2.
:::

## The big idea

Two-Stage TMLE separates *missing-data adjustment* from *between-arm
comparison*:

| Stage | What it does | Independent unit | Estimator |
|-------|--------------|------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Adjust for missing outcomes / mediated missingness **within each cluster** | Individual | TMLE via `ltmle` |
| 2 | Compare cluster-level endpoints **between arms** | Cluster | TMLE with Adaptive Pre-specification |

The separation is the whole point. Stage 1 handles the messy individual-
level data without trying to estimate the effect; Stage 2 sees clean
cluster-level data and runs the comparison with maximum efficiency.

## Stage 1: within-cluster missing-data adjustment

For each cluster $c$ separately, fit an individual-level TMLE for the
cluster-specific endpoint $Y^c$. Two variants:

### Foundational (missing outcomes)

Used when the outcome itself is differentially measured — e.g., viral
suppression status known only for those who returned for follow-up.

$$
Y^c \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\bigl[\mathbb{E}(Y \mid \Delta = 1, W, M)\bigr]
$$

where $\Delta$ is the outcome-measurement indicator and $(W, M)$ are
baseline plus post-baseline covariates. One $\mathrm{ltmle}$ call per
cluster, returning one cluster-level number. See
[Chapter 5](05_R_foundational.qmd) for hands-on code.

### Mediated (the multilevel-mediation-missing problem)

Used when *eligibility / risk status* is what's partially missing,
mediated by post-baseline screening — e.g., HIV risk status known only
for those who got screened.

$$
Y^c \;=\; \frac{\mathbb{P}(Y_2 = 1)}{\mathbb{E}\!\bigl[\mathbb{P}(Y_1 = 1 \mid M = 1, W)\bigr]}
$$

A **ratio** estimator: numerator empirical, denominator TMLE. See
[Chapter 6](06_R_mediated.qmd) for hands-on code.

## Stage 2: cluster-level TMLE with Adaptive Pre-specification

Hand the $J$ cluster-level endpoints to a cluster-level TMLE that
compares treated to control:

$$
\Psi \;=\; \mathbb{E}\bigl[Y^c(1) - Y^c(0)\bigr]
$$

**Adaptive Pre-specification** (APS) picks the adjustment variable that
*minimises the CV variance* of the resulting TMLE, while preserving
Type-I error control [@balzer2016aps]. With few clusters, APS routinely
yields 4–6× efficiency gains over an unadjusted estimator.

## Why this is doubly robust and efficient

Both stages plug in machine-learning (`SuperLearner`) candidate fits.
Stage 1 is doubly robust in the outcome regression and the
$\Delta$-mechanism / $M$-mechanism. Stage 2 is doubly robust in the
cluster-level outcome regression and the (known) randomization
distribution.

When both are estimated well, Two-Stage TMLE is **efficient** — it
achieves the smallest asymptotic variance among regular, asymptotically
linear estimators.

## On variance and finite-sample inference

Stage 2's variance is computed from the cluster-level influence curve
and **treats Stage 1's endpoint as a fixed observation at the cluster
level**. This is justified asymptotically when within-cluster sample
sizes are large; see Box 2 of @balzer2021twostage. Empirically, Nakato
(2026) verifies acceptable coverage at $N_c \approx 200$ per cluster,
$J \in \{20, 30, 50, 70\}$ — well within the regime of most HIV
prevention CRTs.

## Where this leaves us

You now know enough to run real code:

- [Chapter 5](05_R_foundational.qmd) — `stage1_tmle()` + `stage2_tmle()` on
  a CRT with missing outcomes.
- [Chapter 6](06_R_mediated.qmd) — `stage1_mediated()` + `stage2_tmle()`
  on a CRT with the multilevel-mediation-missing problem.

## Resources

::: {.callout-tip}
**Further reading**

- @balzer2021twostage — the foundational paper.
- @balzer2016aps — Adaptive Pre-specification.
- @nakato2026mediated — the mediated Stage 1 estimator.
- @benitez2023crt — comparator methods.
:::
